claims.summary: Release node binaries must not link classical curve, pairing, or trusted-setup verifier surfaces.
assumptions: The release binary under target/release/hegemon-node is the binary that operators deploy.
The audit pattern set remains synchronized with the forbidden primitive policy.

claims.component: cargo dependency graph and formal-core checker toolchain
claims.summary: Every cargo-audit advisory or yanked crate in release code or formal-core tooling must be removed or explicitly waived with policy metadata.
assumptions: cargo-audit is available in release CI.
Waiver metadata is reviewed before release tagging.
The standalone formal-core checker lockfile is audited separately from the root workspace lockfile.

claims.summary: The Lean transaction-balance kernel proves native-delta and stablecoin-rule extraction for executable balance validation, and generated Lean vectors are checked against production TransactionWitness balance slot and validation behavior.
assumptions: The Lean NoteSummary model represents transaction balance asset ids and values, not note commitment hashing, Merkle hash-function security, ciphertext hashes, or hash-derived nullifiers.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover TransactionWitness::balance_slots and TransactionWitness::validate balance accept/reject behavior for native fees, non-native conservation, stablecoin issuance, and slot overflow cases.
The theorem covers executable balance-slot and validation semantics, not full AIR/proof-system soundness or every witness/public-input field.

claims.summary: The Lean Merkle-path kernel proves executable path-depth, position-bit orientation, computed-root acceptance, and wrong-root/length rejection facts, and generated Lean vectors are checked against the production MerklePath fold helper used by transaction membership verification.
assumptions: The Lean Digest model and generated vectors use an abstract mock node combiner to prove and check Merkle path control flow; Poseidon2 merkle_node collision resistance and implementation equivalence are separate cryptographic assumptions.
The SmallWood native membership precheck rejects paths whose sibling length differs from MERKLE_TREE_DEPTH before calling MerklePath::verify.

The theorem covers path folding, position-bit orientation, depth admission, and root equality semantics, not note commitment hashing, nullifier derivation, ciphertext hashing, or full AIR/proof-system soundness.

claims.summary: The Lean public-input shape kernel proves fixed vector widths, boolean flags, inactive padding zeroing, active nullifier/commitment nonzero checks, nonempty transaction admission, canonical balance-slot asset ordering, and stablecoin asset presence, and generated Lean vectors are checked against production TransactionPublicInputsP3::validate.
assumptions: The Lean Digest model and generated vectors use abstract one-limb nonzero/zero digests; they do not prove Poseidon2, note commitment, nullifier, or ciphertext hash derivation security.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover the verifier-facing shape/admission rules enforced by TransactionPublicInputsP3::validate, including malformed flags, inactive nonzero fields, active zero fields, empty transactions, balance-slot ordering, and stablecoin asset membership.

The theorem covers public-input shape admission, not statement-hash preimage binding, which is covered separately by formal.transaction-statement-hash-binding, STARK verifier soundness, SmallWood soundness, or complete Rust refinement.

claims.summary: The Lean public-input binding kernel proves executable accept/reject facts for the verifier seam that combines TransactionPublicInputs with SerializedStarkInputs before constructing TransactionPublicInputsP3, and generated Lean vectors are checked against production transaction_public_inputs_p3_from_parts.
assumptions: The Lean model treats 48-byte commitments as abstract digest identifiers; it does not prove Poseidon2/BLAKE3 implementation equivalence or digest collision resistance.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover merkle-root, fee, signed value-balance, balance-slot asset list, stablecoin enable/asset/version/issuance/hash/oracle/attestation binding, and the legacy empty serialized balance-slot fallback.

The theorem covers the public/serialized binding predicate used before verifier public-input construction, not transaction statement-hash preimage binding, which is covered separately by formal.transaction-statement-hash-binding, STARK verifier soundness, SmallWood soundness, or complete Rust refinement.

claims.summary: The Lean statement-hash kernel proves executable facts for the exact transaction statement-hash preimage grammar, and generated Lean vectors are checked byte-for-byte against the shared Rust preimage/hash helper used by transaction receipts and consensus tx-leaf binding.
assumptions: The Lean model treats 48-byte commitments as abstract byte arrays generated from deterministic seeds; it does not prove Poseidon2/BLAKE3 implementation equivalence or digest collision resistance.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover the statement-hash domain separator, merkle root, padded nullifier/commitment/ciphertext-hash lists, little-endian fee/version/stablecoin fields, two's-complement signed value-balance and stablecoin issuance encoding, and rejection for over-wide vectors or invalid signed-magnitude flags.

The theorem covers the byte preimage grammar and Rust BLAKE3 invocation over that checked preimage, not BLAKE3 cryptographic security, note commitment hashing, nullifier derivation, ciphertext hash derivation, STARK verifier soundness, SmallWood soundness, or complete Rust refinement.

claims.summary: The Lean nullifier-state kernel proves zero, pending, and spent nullifier rejection, and generated Lean vectors are checked against the production protocol-shielded-pool helper used by the native node.
assumptions: The Lean Nullifier equality model represents equality of canonical 48-byte nullifiers.
Generated Lean conformance vectors cover protocol-shielded-pool NullifierState stage/import behavior.
The theorem covers native nullifier set state transitions, not full transaction balance conservation or AIR/proof soundness.

claims.summary: The Lean fork-choice kernel proves deterministic work, height, and hash tie-break ordering, and generated Lean vectors are checked against the production helper used by PoW consensus and native block import.
assumptions: The Lean ForkChoiceTip work model represents the ordering result of BigUint work and fixed-width Work48 comparisons, not PoW admission arithmetic or SHA-256 header-hash implementation equivalence.
Generated Lean conformance vectors cover the consensus::fork_choice helper used by PowConsensus and native block import.
The theorem covers deterministic two-tip fork-choice ordering, not every network partition, timestamp edge case, or long-range adversarial mining schedule.

claims.summary: The Lean PoW admission kernel proves compact-target rejection, strict timestamp admission, hash-threshold rejection, and fixed-width cumulative-work behavior; generated Lean vectors are checked against consensus admission and light-client Work48 helpers.
assumptions: The Lean model represents arithmetic over already-computed PoW hash values; header preimage serialization is covered separately by consensus.header-preimage-binding, while SHA-256 implementation equivalence remains outside this claim.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid boundary hashes, height mismatch, pow_bits mismatch, timestamp equal-parent rejection, median-time-past rejection, future-skew rejection, zero-mantissa target rejection, exponent-over-32 target rejection, insufficient-work rejection, cumulative-work mismatch, and Work48 overflow.

The theorem covers executable target/timestamp/work admission semantics, not difficulty-retarget economics, network orphan policy, SHA-256 implementation equivalence, or long-range adversarial mining schedules.

claims.component: consensus block header signing and work-hash byte binding
claims.summary: The Lean header-preimage kernel proves executable byte-layout facts for consensus signing preimages and full header preimages, and generated Lean vectors are checked against production BlockHeader preimage helpers used by miner signature verification and PoW work hashing.
assumptions: The Lean model treats fixed-width roots, commitments, hashes, signatures, bitmaps, and PoW nonces as byte arrays and proves the byte grammar around those values, not their cryptographic derivation.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover the signing preimage domain, fixed field order, little-endian integer encodings, BFT bitmap tag/length encoding, PoW seal tag/nonce/pow_bits encoding, absent auth tags, and signing-preimage independence from signature, bitmap, and PoW seal payloads.

The theorem covers executable header preimage serialization only; it does not prove SHA-256 collision resistance, SHA-256 implementation equivalence, ML-DSA unforgeability, semantic correctness of the header field values, retarget economics, or network finality.

claims.component: PoW miner identity and signature-shape admission
claims.summary: The Lean miner-identity kernel proves that PoW headers are accepted only when they carry no BFT signature bitmap, bind a registered miner key, carry exactly one ML-DSA-65-sized signature, parse the signature bytes, and pass the real verifier result; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production PowConsensus helper.
assumptions: The Lean model treats ML-DSA signature parsing and verification as boolean results from the production cryptography implementation.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover the accepted case, forbidden BFT bitmap rejection, unregistered miner rejection, missing and oversized signature rejection, invalid signature byte rejection, and failed signature-verification rejection.

The theorem covers executable miner identity and signature admission ordering, not ML-DSA unforgeability, ML-DSA implementation equivalence, key-registration governance, or miner key compromise. Header signing-hash byte grammar is covered separately by consensus.header-preimage-binding.

claims.summary: The Lean version-policy kernel proves executable admission facts for initial protocol versions, activation-height boundaries, retirement-height boundaries, same-height retirement precedence, duplicate initial bindings, and first unsupported transaction-version reporting; generated Lean vectors are checked against the VersionSchedule helper used by PoW and BFT block import.
assumptions: The Lean model represents version bindings as circuit/crypto pairs and schedule events as explicit activation and retirement lists.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover accepted initial versions, unknown-version rejection, pre-activation rejection, activation-boundary acceptance, pre-retirement acceptance, retirement-boundary rejection, same-height retirement precedence, first-unsupported transaction ordering, and duplicate initial binding deduplication.

The theorem covers executable VersionSchedule admission semantics, not social upgrade governance, protocol manifest correctness, proof-system soundness for each version, or full native-node equivalence.

claims.summary: The Lean header-MMR kernel proves executable opening-shape facts for peak ranges, peak selection, leaf bounds, sibling-count requirements, local-index derivation, and left/right path orientation; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production helper used by standalone and long-range bridge MMR opening verification.
assumptions: The Lean model represents header MMR opening shape and path orientation over already-computed header and sibling hashes.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid singleton, multi-peak, and four-leaf orientation cases plus context mismatch, leaf-out-of-range, empty leaf-set, peak-count mismatch, and sibling-count mismatch rejection.

The theorem covers executable MMR opening shape semantics, not SHA-256 implementation equivalence, MMR parent/root hash security, FlyClient sampling soundness, or complete long-range light-client soundness.

claims.component: bridge header MMR parent/root transcript binding
claims.summary: The Lean header-MMR transcript kernel proves executable byte-layout facts for parent-node and peak-root hash preimages; generated Lean vectors are checked byte-for-byte against the production helpers hashed by bridge MMR construction and verification.
assumptions: The Lean model represents exact BLAKE3 input bytes for already-computed header hash, sibling hash, peak hash, level, leaf-count, and peak-count values.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover the parent domain, u32 little-endian level encoding including the max-u32 case, left/right child order, root domain, u64 little-endian leaf count, u32 little-endian peak count, empty roots, ordered peak concatenation, and reversed-peak transcript distinction.

The theorem covers executable byte preimage grammar and Rust conformance to that grammar, not BLAKE3 cryptographic security, BLAKE3 implementation equivalence, SHA-256 header-hash implementation equivalence, MMR collision resistance, full MMR hash binding, FlyClient sampling soundness, or complete long-range light-client soundness.

claims.summary: The Lean long-range bridge kernel proves executable admission facts for verifier hash binding, message-count binding, header/MMR length shape, message-header ordering, message index/source binding, FlyClient sample height/opening-index agreement, confirmation policy, tip-work policy, and claimed-output agreement; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production helper called by the bridge long-range verifier.
assumptions: The Lean model represents cheap long-range bridge proof control-flow over already-computed header hashes, sample indices, work values, and message metadata.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover verifier hash mismatch, header message-count mismatch, header MMR length mismatch, stale tip/message ordering, message MMR opening leaf mismatch, out-of-bounds message index, message source chain/height mismatch, FlyClient sample count/height/opening-index mismatch, under-confirmed outputs, insufficient tip work, claimed-output mismatch, and output-omitted guest verification.

The theorem covers executable structural admission semantics, not SHA-256 implementation equivalence, PoW hash security, MMR hash binding, bridge message-root hashing, FlyClient sampling soundness, external-chain verifier behavior, checkpoint-output byte grammar, or complete long-range light-client soundness. Header MMR opening shape and path orientation are covered separately by bridge.header-mmr-opening-shape. Header MMR parent/root transcript bytes are covered separately by bridge.header-mmr-transcript-binding. FlyClient transcript/index derivation is covered separately by bridge.flyclient-sampling-transcript. Checkpoint output canonical/journal bytes are covered separately by bridge.checkpoint-output-binding.

claims.summary: The Lean checkpoint-output kernel proves the exact domain-separated canonical preimage and fixed-width journal tuple grammar for BridgeCheckpointOutputV1; generated Lean vectors are checked byte-for-byte against production consensus-light-client encoding and decoding helpers.
assumptions: The Lean model represents exact bytes for already-computed source chain ids, rules hashes, header hashes, work values, message roots, message hashes, nonce, confirmation count, and minimum-work policy.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover the 35-byte canonical domain, 439-byte canonical preimage, 404-byte authenticated journal tuple, field order, u64/u128/u32 little-endian scalar encodings including max scalar cases, decode/re-encode round trips, and fixed-length rejection for truncated or trailing journal bytes.

The theorem covers byte grammar and Rust conformance to that grammar, not BLAKE3 cryptographic security, BLAKE3 implementation equivalence, bridge message-root/hash derivation, external verifier behavior, or complete bridge light-client soundness.

claims.component: bridge FlyClient sampling transcript and index derivation
claims.summary: The Lean FlyClient kernel proves executable transcript-byte and digest-prefix reduction facts for deterministic sample index derivation; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production helpers called by the bridge long-range verifier before sample openings are verified.
assumptions: The Lean model represents the sample transcript byte grammar and digest-prefix-to-height arithmetic over already-computed BLAKE3 digest prefixes.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover exact domain/preimage bytes, u64/u32 little-endian bounds and sample indices, modulo reduction, max-u64 digest prefixes, duplicate sample preservation, sample-count truncation, zero sample count, and invalid empty/reversed ranges.

The theorem covers executable FlyClient sample-index derivation semantics, not BLAKE3 cryptographic security or implementation equivalence, probabilistic FlyClient sampling soundness, MMR hash binding, PoW hash security, or complete long-range light-client soundness.

claims.summary: The Lean proof-policy kernel proves the shipped non-empty block proof payload shape: self-contained mode, transaction artifacts, transaction validity claims, a proven batch, recursive blocks with no legacy commitment-proof bytes or receipt-root payload, and a required block artifact; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production consensus verifier helper.
assumptions: The Lean model represents the consensus proof payload availability and route-shape decision before cryptographic artifact verification.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover empty-block proof payload rejection, missing transaction artifacts, artifact-count mismatch, legacy InlineRequired rejection, missing proven batches, missing transaction validity claims, legacy inline batches, recursive commitment-proof byte rejection, recursive receipt-root payload rejection, missing recursive block artifacts, and receipt-root payload requirements.

The theorem covers executable proof policy admission semantics, not proven-batch binding, recursive proof cryptographic soundness, transaction leaf proof soundness, aggregation V5 header admission, DA root derivation, or native backend security. Proven-batch binding is covered separately by consensus.proven-batch-binding. Aggregation V5 header admission is covered separately by consensus.aggregation-v5-header-admission.

claims.component: data-availability root byte grammar and shard-count admission
claims.summary: The Lean DA-root kernel proves executable byte and arithmetic facts for transaction ciphertext blob serialization, DA parameter rejection, data/parity/total shard counts, BLAKE3 leaf/node preimage domains, and Merkle proof-step orientation; generated Lean vectors are checked against production consensus and state-da helpers.
assumptions: The Lean model represents the deterministic bytes and shard-count arithmetic that consensus uses before hashing and comparing a block's DA root.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover empty and multi-transaction DA blobs, empty ciphertext length-prefixing, leaf preimages for index zero and max u32, ordered and reversed node preimages, empty/partial/max shard counts, zero chunk-size rejection, zero sample-count rejection, too-many-shards rejection, and even/odd Merkle proof-step orientation.

The theorem covers executable DA byte grammar and shard-count admission only. It does not prove Reed-Solomon erasure-code correctness, BLAKE3 security or implementation equivalence, chunk network availability, sampling probability, ciphertext sidecar availability, or complete block proof validity.

claims.summary: The Lean native tx-leaf admission kernel proves executable fail-closed facts for missing proof envelopes, tx_leaf envelope kind, native verifier-profile agreement, artifact size caps, receipt verifier-profile agreement, expected artifact-hash checks, and cache receipt plus public transaction-view agreement before expensive backend verification or cache reuse; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production consensus helper.
assumptions: The Lean model represents the cheap consensus admission table that runs before native tx-leaf backend verification or cache reuse.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid uncached admission, valid cache hits, missing envelopes, wrong artifact kinds, envelope verifier-profile mismatch, artifact size cap rejection, receipt verifier-profile mismatch, expected artifact hash mismatch, absent expected-hash skipping, cache receipt mismatch, cache transaction-view mismatch, and exact-size-limit acceptance.

The theorem covers executable envelope/profile/size/hash/cache admission only. It does not prove native tx-leaf artifact byte parsing, statement-hash preimage binding, native backend proof soundness, BLAKE3 artifact-hash security or implementation equivalence, or complete block proof validity. Native tx-leaf artifact wire grammar and transaction statement-hash binding are covered separately.

claims.summary: The Lean proven-batch binding kernel proves the executable acceptance predicate for proof route compatibility, tx-count binding, statement-commitment binding, DA-root binding, nonzero DA chunks, block-artifact envelope kind/profile agreement, and recursive-block receipt-root exclusion; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production consensus helper.
assumptions: The Lean model represents the cheap proven-batch binding gate after DA root computation succeeds and before recursive block or receipt-root proof verification.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid recursive_block_v2 and receipt_root bindings plus incompatible routes, tx-count mismatch, statement-commitment mismatch, DA-root mismatch, zero DA chunk count, artifact kind mismatch, artifact verifier-profile mismatch, recursive-block receipt-root payload rejection, missing-artifact envelope skipping, recursive_block_v1 route compatibility, and tx_leaf route rejection.

The theorem covers executable proven-batch binding only. It does not prove DA erasure-code soundness, BLAKE3 security, recursive proof cryptographic soundness, transaction leaf proof soundness, aggregation V5 header admission, native backend security, or complete block proof validity. DA byte grammar and shard-count binding are covered separately by consensus.da-root-byte-binding.

claims.summary: The Lean recursive-block admission kernel proves executable fail-closed facts for recursive_block_v1/v2 envelope kind, verifier-profile agreement, artifact decoding, recursive header-version agreement, tx-count agreement, statement-commitment agreement, public replay agreement, and direct registry-verifier semantic-replay rejection; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production consensus verifier helpers.
assumptions: The Lean model represents the cheap consensus admission and binding table around recursive_block_v1/v2 artifact verification after the ordered tx_leaf stream has already been verified, plus the rule that the generic registry ArtifactVerifier path must reject direct recursive-block verification because it lacks verified-record semantic replay inputs.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid recursive_block_v2 and recursive_block_v1 product-path admission cases plus wrong artifact kind, verifier-profile mismatch, artifact decode failure, recursive header-version mismatch, tx-count mismatch, statement-commitment mismatch, public replay mismatch, rejection-precedence for wrong kind before decode failure, and direct recursive_block_v1/v2 registry-verifier rejection with requires_semantic_replay.

The theorem covers executable recursive-block envelope/decode/header/count/statement/public-replay admission and direct registry-verifier semantic-replay rejection only. It does not prove native tx-leaf proof soundness, recursive proof cryptographic soundness, public replay semantic correctness beyond the separate recursive-public-replay field-propagation claim, BLAKE3 security or implementation equivalence, aggregation STARK soundness, or complete block proof validity. Proven-batch binding, native tx-leaf admission, native tx-leaf artifact wire grammar, and aggregation V5 header admission are covered separately.

claims.summary: The Lean recursive public replay kernel proves executable facts for v1/v2 recursive-block public replay: contiguous tx-index admission, rejection of gaps/duplicates/decreasing order, exact semantic-field propagation into public tuples, v1 message-root carriage, v2 message-root omission, and public tuple byte lengths; generated Lean vectors are checked against block-recursion production helpers.
assumptions: The Lean model represents the public replay field-mapping and tx-index admission table around recursive_block_v1/v2 public tuple construction, not recursive proof cryptographic soundness.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid recursive_block_v1/v2 zero-based replay, the production helper behavior for a nonzero contiguous start, gap rejection, duplicate-index rejection, decreasing-order rejection, v1/v2 serialized public byte lengths, and semantic-root/commitment propagation.

The theorem covers executable public replay field propagation and tx-index ordering only. It does not prove BLAKE3/fold_digest48 security or implementation equivalence, recursive proof cryptographic soundness, correctness of native tx-leaf proofs, correctness of block semantic inputs derived from native state beyond the separate consensus.recursive-semantic-inputs source-mapping claim, or complete block proof validity.

claims.summary: The Lean recursive semantic-input kernel proves executable admission and source-mapping facts for the recursive-block semantic tuple: nonempty block/nullifier/DA admission, expected statement commitment, parent and applied commitment-tree roots, kernel roots derived from those roots, nonzero-nullifier root source, DA root source, header message-root source, and recursive tree-state commitments; generated Lean vectors are checked against the private consensus semantic derivation helper.
assumptions: The Lean model represents the cheap source-mapping and rejection-order table around recursive_block_v1/v2 semantic tuple construction, not recursive proof cryptographic soundness.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid default-parent and nondefault-parent cases, empty-block rejection, excessive-nullifier rejection, zero-nullifier rejection, missing-nonzero-nullifier rejection, duplicate-nullifier rejection, DA-encoding rejection, and duplicate-before-DA rejection precedence.

The theorem covers executable semantic field source binding only. It does not prove BLAKE3, DA erasure-code, Merkle tree hash, kernel-root hash, nullifier-root hash, or recursive-state-commitment cryptographic security or implementation equivalence, native tx-leaf proof soundness, recursive proof cryptographic soundness, or complete block proof validity.

claims.summary: The Lean block tree-transition kernel proves the executable acceptance predicate for proof starting-root agreement, commitment application success, proof ending-root agreement, accepted applied-root return, and rejection precedence; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production consensus tree-transition verifier used after block proof verification.
assumptions: The Lean model represents commitment-tree roots as abstract digest identifiers and commitment application as a checked success/failure bit; it does not prove Merkle hash security or implementation equivalence.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover empty valid transitions, one-commitment valid transitions, zero commitment filtering, starting-root mismatch rejection, commitment application failure, ending-root mismatch rejection, starting-root-before-application-failure precedence, and application-failure-before-ending-root precedence.

The theorem covers executable block commitment-tree transition control flow only. It does not prove note commitment correctness, BLAKE3 or Poseidon2 implementation equivalence, recursive proof cryptographic soundness, native tx-leaf proof soundness, DA soundness, or complete block proof validity.

claims.component: receipt-root proof payload and block-artifact admission
claims.summary: The Lean receipt-root admission kernel proves executable fail-closed facts for receipt-root payload leaf/receipt agreement, tx-validity claim agreement, transaction artifact availability, receipt_root envelope kind, native verifier-profile agreement, artifact size caps, tx-artifact count agreement, statement-commitment agreement, and verified leaf-count agreement; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production consensus helpers.
assumptions: The Lean model represents the cheap consensus admission and binding tables around receipt-root block artifact verification, including the checks that run before native tx-leaf verification, before backend root verification, and after root metadata is returned.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid payload/artifact/statement/metadata cases plus payload leaf-count mismatch, receipt-count mismatch, missing tx-validity claims, payload receipt mismatch, missing transaction artifacts, wrong artifact kind, verifier-profile mismatch, artifact size cap rejection, tx-artifact count mismatch, statement-commitment mismatch, verified leaf-count mismatch, and exact-size-limit acceptance.

The theorem covers executable receipt-root payload/envelope/size/count/statement/metadata admission only. It does not prove native tx-leaf proof soundness, native receipt-root byte parsing, native receipt-root cryptographic fold soundness, backend transcript challenge derivation, BLAKE3 security or implementation equivalence, or complete block proof validity. Native tx-leaf admission, native receipt-root wire grammar, and proven-batch binding are covered separately.

claims.component: self-contained aggregation proof V5 header admission
claims.summary: The Lean aggregation V5 kernel proves executable header-admission facts for version, format, packed-public-values encoding, statement-commitment binding, child/fan-in bounds, subtree tx-count binding, tree-level/root-level checks, binary-minimum merge fan-in, and packed public-input length agreement; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production consensus aggregation V5 header helper.
assumptions: The Lean model represents the cheap aggregation V5 payload header gate before representative child proof decoding and STARK batch verification.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid singleton leaf and merge headers plus bad version, bad proof format, bad public-values encoding, bad statement-commitment length, statement-commitment mismatch, child-count underflow/overflow, subtree tx-count mismatch, tree-level mismatch, root-level overflow, leaf fan-in overflow, merge fan-in mismatch, and packed public-input length mismatch.

The theorem covers executable aggregation V5 header admission only. It does not prove postcard or zstd implementation equivalence, representative child proof decoding, transaction proof soundness, aggregation STARK soundness, packed public-value cryptographic binding, or complete block proof validity.

claims.summary: The Lean supply-accounting kernel proves checked native supply-digest increase/decrease, no-coinbase native no-op behavior, and native overflow rejection; generated Lean vectors are checked against consensus CoinbaseData/update_supply_digest and native-node coinbase supply helpers.
assumptions: The Lean supply model represents 128-bit supply_digest arithmetic as Nat values bounded by u128::MAX and native coinbase fee totals as u64-bounded amounts.
Generated Lean vectors cover consensus minted + fees - burns behavior, burn underflow, supply overflow, no-coinbase native no-op blocks, coinbase fee inclusion, and native supply overflow rejection.
The theorem covers executable supply accounting and checked Rust conformance, not full proof-system soundness, monetary policy economics, or every wallet-side reward construction path.

claims.summary: The Lean supply-chain invariant kernel proves that accepted claimed supply steps replay to the same final supply as the executable expected-delta sequence; generated Lean chain vectors are checked against the production consensus supply transition helper used by PoW validation.
assumptions: The Lean model represents a chain prefix as an ordered list of claimed supply steps carrying minted amount, signed fees, burns, and the claimed post-block supply digest.
Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid multi-step replay, claimed-supply counterfeiting, burn underflow, and supply overflow rejection.

The theorem covers executable supply-chain replay and claimed supply matching. It does not prove transaction proof soundness, privacy, hash implementation equivalence, monetary policy economics beyond the current subsidy/delta helpers, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.summary: The Lean native coinbase-accounting admission kernel proves the executable table for zero-or-one coinbase actions, checked subsidy-plus-fee reward computation, missing amount rejection, reward mismatch rejection, and the intentional no-coinbase fee-burn policy; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production native helper used by block import and mining.
assumptions: The Lean model reuses the Consensus.Supply subsidy and checked u64 addition model for expected native coinbase amounts.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover no-coinbase acceptance, valid subsidy-only and fee-inclusive coinbase rewards, multiple coinbase rejection, fee-total overflow rejection, reward overflow rejection, missing amount rejection, reward mismatch rejection, and rejection precedence.

The theorem covers executable native coinbase accounting admission only. It does not prove encrypted coinbase note construction, ciphertext hash binding, monetary policy economics beyond the current subsidy helper, full action payload validation, or proof-system soundness.

claims.summary: The Lean native coinbase action payload-admission kernel proves the executable table for decoded coinbase reward-note amount, commitment agreement/nonzero checks, ciphertext byte caps, and ciphertext hash/size metadata agreement; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production native helper used by mempool and block action validation.
assumptions: The Lean model represents decoded coinbase payload/public metadata admission after action route and scope admission.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid payloads, exact ciphertext-cap acceptance, zero amount, commitment mismatch, zero commitment, ciphertext oversize, ciphertext hash mismatch, ciphertext size mismatch, and rejection precedence.

The theorem covers executable decoded coinbase payload binding only. It does not prove encrypted note confidentiality, ciphertext hash cryptographic security or implementation equivalence, reward accounting, complete action decoding, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.component: native mempool and sidecar resource-budget admission
claims.summary: The Lean native resource-budget admission kernel proves the executable saturated byte-budget predicates for pending-action mempool admission and staged proof replacement; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production native helpers used by action staging and da_submitProofs.
assumptions: The Lean model represents Rust usize saturating_add and saturating_sub behavior for already-computed encoded action bytes and staged proof byte totals.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover empty and exact-limit mempool acceptance, mempool over-limit rejection, saturated mempool totals, staged proof exact-limit acceptance, staged proof over-limit rejection, replacement subtracting the existing proof bytes, existing-byte overcount saturation, and saturated staged-proof totals.

The theorem covers executable byte-budget admission only. It does not prove RPC authentication, public exposure safety for unsafe methods, sled durability, proof byte parsing, native tx-leaf proof soundness, DA sidecar availability, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.summary: The Lean native sidecar upload admission kernel proves deterministic request-count, staged-capacity, proof metadata, and decoded proof-byte admission for da_submitCiphertexts and da_submitProofs; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production native helpers used by the unsafe DA staging RPCs and startup proof reload.
assumptions: The Lean model represents decoded upload-admission predicates after JSON field discovery and bounded byte parsing, with binding-hash presence/shape, proof-field presence, and decoded proof byte length represented as explicit inputs.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover empty/exact/over-limit ciphertext and proof request counts, new-vs-replacement staged ciphertext and proof capacity, proof binding-hash missing/invalid precedence, missing proof fields, empty proofs, exact proof-cap acceptance, and oversized decoded proof rejection.

The theorem covers executable native sidecar upload admission only. It does not prove RPC authentication, public exposure safety for unsafe methods, base64/hex parser correctness, ciphertext hash cryptographic security or implementation equivalence, sled durability, DA sidecar availability, native tx-leaf proof soundness, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.summary: The Lean candidate-artifact admission kernel proves the executable native action/artifact table for no state-delta smuggling, payload presence, schema, tx-count and DA-chunk bounds, shipped recursive_block_v2 route/profile, empty legacy proof fields, missing/empty recursive payload rejection, and recursive proof byte caps; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production helper used by native candidate action/artifact validation.
assumptions: The Lean model treats candidate-artifact route identity and deep artifact fields as already decoded production predicates; it does not prove SCALE decoding or verifier-profile digest construction.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid candidate artifacts, exact recursive proof size limit acceptance, state-delta smuggling, missing payloads, schema mismatch, zero and oversized tx counts, zero DA chunks, wrong proof mode/kind/profile, legacy commitment-proof and receipt-root payload rejection, missing/empty recursive proof payload, proof oversize, and rejection precedence.

The theorem covers executable native candidate-artifact admission only. It does not prove recursive proof cryptographic soundness, native tx-leaf soundness, DA soundness, statement commitment correctness, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.component: native candidate artifact block coupling admission
claims.summary: The Lean candidate-artifact coupling kernel proves the executable native block table requiring zero candidate artifacts when a block has no shielded transfers, and exactly one candidate artifact whose tx_count matches the shielded transfer count when a block is non-empty; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production helper used before recursive block artifact verification.
assumptions: The Lean model treats shielded transfer counts, decoded candidate-artifact counts, and candidate tx_count agreement as production-derived values. Route scope, payload presence, and candidate-artifact shape are covered by separate native action-scope and candidate-artifact admission claims.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover empty block acceptance, matching single-candidate acceptance for one and multiple transfers, orphan candidate rejection, missing candidate rejection, multiple candidate rejection, tx_count mismatch rejection, and rejection precedence.

The theorem covers only cheap block-level transfer/candidate coupling before recursive verification. It does not prove recursive proof cryptographic soundness, native tx-leaf proof soundness, DA-root correctness, statement-commitment correctness, candidate selection fairness, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.summary: The Lean block-artifact binding kernel proves the executable native equality gates that bind decoded tx-leaf public fields to shielded transfer action metadata, bind ciphertext payload hashes to action ciphertext hashes, and bind recursive candidate artifacts to the computed DA root, tx statement commitment, and verified recursive state root; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production helpers used by native block artifact verification.
assumptions: The Lean model treats decoded tx-leaf public fields, computed ciphertext hashes, computed DA roots, computed tx statement commitments, and verified recursive state roots as production-derived predicates. The hash functions and recursive verifier are covered by separate claims and residual assumptions.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid tx-leaf/action binding, nullifier/commitment/ciphertext-hash/version/ciphertext-payload-hash mismatches, candidate DA-root mismatch, tx-statement commitment mismatch, recursive state-root mismatch, and rejection precedence.

The theorem covers only deterministic equality-gate admission after candidate-artifact coupling and tx-leaf decoding. It does not prove recursive proof cryptographic soundness, native tx-leaf proof soundness, DA-root hash security/equivalence, statement-commitment hash security/equivalence, ciphertext hash security/equivalence, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.summary: The Lean action-ordering kernel proves that native shielded transfer order is the nondecreasing lexicographic order of computed transfer keys while non-transfer actions are ignored; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production native order predicate used by block import.
assumptions: The Lean model treats transfer order keys as already-computed canonical 32-byte values and does not prove BLAKE2 key derivation from binding hashes and nullifiers.
Generated Lean conformance vectors cover empty blocks, single transfers, ascending keys, equal keys, descending rejection, tail inversion rejection, and ignored non-transfer actions.
The theorem covers the executable transfer-key ordering predicate, not full block action payload validation, tx leaf proof soundness, or action_order_key hash-function equivalence.

claims.component: native block action hash and count admission
claims.summary: The Lean action-hash admission kernel proves count agreement, embedded action-hash agreement, and duplicate action-hash rejection ordering; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production native admission helper used by block decode and import.
assumptions: The Lean model treats action-count agreement, pending_action_hash agreement, and action-hash uniqueness as Boolean predicates; it does not prove BLAKE3 collision resistance or SCALE encoding implementation equivalence.
Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid admission, action payload count mismatch, action hash mismatch, duplicate action hash rejection, and hash-mismatch-before-duplicate precedence.

The theorem covers the executable admission table used before native block-action semantic validation, not full action payload validation, action_order_key derivation, tx leaf proof soundness, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.component: native action route and public metadata scope admission
claims.summary: The Lean action-scope admission kernel proves the executable table that routes native actions into bridge, candidate-artifact, coinbase, or shielded-transfer validation only after candidate-artifact payload scoping, route-specific state-delta scope, candidate payload presence, and transfer public metadata shape predicates pass; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production native admission helper used by mempool and block validation.
assumptions: The Lean model treats route identity, route-specific state-delta scope, candidate payload presence, and transfer public metadata shape as Boolean predicates computed by production Rust; it does not prove SCALE decoding, bridge receipt verification, candidate artifact proof soundness, coinbase ciphertext binding, transaction binding-hash validation, or proof-system soundness.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid bridge, candidate-artifact, coinbase, and transfer routes; wrong candidate-artifact payload route; bridge, candidate, coinbase, unsupported, and transfer-scope rejection; missing candidate payload; and wrong-candidate-route-before-bridge-scope precedence.

The theorem covers the executable route/scope admission table called before deep action payload validation in native mempool and block import, not complete native action semantics, action ordering, nullifier replay, bridge message replay, tx leaf proof soundness, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.component: native decoded bridge action payload admission
claims.summary: The Lean bridge-payload admission kernel proves the executable decoded bridge action table for bridge-family routing, no shielded state-delta smuggling, supported bridge action IDs, nonempty outbound payloads, inbound proof-receipt presence, inbound replay-key/message binding, Hegemon destination binding, inbound payload-hash binding, and rejection precedence; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production native helper used by bridge action validation.
assumptions: The Lean model treats SCALE decoding, bridge_payload_hash computation, route identity, and state-delta scanning as production-derived predicates; it proves the decoded pre-receipt admission table and rejection precedence.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid outbound, inbound, and verifier-registration payloads; non-bridge actions; state-delta smuggling; unsupported bridge actions; empty outbound payloads; empty inbound proof receipts; inbound replay-key, destination, and payload-hash mismatches; and not-bridge/state-delta/inbound-precondition precedence.

The theorem covers executable decoded native bridge payload admission only. It does not prove SCALE decoder correctness, bridge payload hash cryptographic security or implementation equivalence, inbound replay-set state transitions, disabled RISC Zero receipt soundness, future PQ bridge receipt soundness, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.component: native decoded shielded transfer payload admission
claims.summary: The Lean transfer-payload admission kernel proves the executable decoded shielded-transfer table for proof presence and size caps, anchor agreement, commitment agreement, inline ciphertext byte caps, ciphertext hash/size metadata agreement, binding-hash agreement, and fee agreement; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production native helper used by mempool and block validation.
assumptions: The Lean model treats SCALE decoding, ciphertext hash derivation, and binding-hash computation as production-derived predicates; it proves the decoded admission table and rejection precedence.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid payloads, exact proof-limit acceptance, missing and oversized proofs, anchor mismatch, commitment mismatch, inline ciphertext oversize, ciphertext hash and size mismatches, binding-hash mismatch, fee mismatch, and proof-missing-before-anchor precedence.

The theorem covers executable decoded native transfer payload admission only. It does not prove SCALE decoder correctness, ciphertext hash cryptographic security or implementation equivalence, StarkVerifier binding-hash cryptographic assumptions, nullifier replay, DA sidecar availability, tx leaf proof soundness, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.summary: The Lean transfer-state admission kernel proves the executable native shielded-transfer state table for known anchors, nullifier replay status, nonzero output commitments, sidecar ciphertext availability, sidecar ciphertext size presence, and staged size agreement; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production helper used by mempool and block validation.
assumptions: The Lean model treats anchor membership, nullifier status, commitment nonzeroness, sidecar route identity, and staged sidecar lookup results as production-derived predicates; it proves the executable admission table and rejection precedence.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid inline and sidecar state admission, unknown anchors, zero/spent/duplicate/pending nullifiers, zero commitments, missing staged ciphertexts, missing staged ciphertext sizes, staged size mismatches, unknown-anchor-before-nullifier precedence, nullifier-before-commitment precedence, and inline transfer independence from sidecar staging.

The theorem covers executable native transfer state admission only. It does not prove commitment-tree hash security, BTreeSet/BTreeMap implementation correctness, ciphertext hash derivation/security, tx leaf proof soundness, recursive block proof soundness, DA durability, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.summary: The Lean block commitment-admission kernel proves the executable table requiring derived tx-count, state-root, kernel-root, nullifier-root, action-root, bridge message-root/count, header-MMR root/length, and supply-digest agreement before native mined, announced, or replayed blocks are accepted; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production native admission helper.
assumptions: The Lean model treats each derived commitment equality as a Boolean predicate; it does not prove commitment-tree hash security, bridge message-root collision resistance, header-MMR hash binding, or BLAKE3/Blake2 implementation equivalence.
Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid admission, every single field mismatch, and rejection precedence when state-root and supply-digest mismatches are both present.

The theorem covers the executable native admission table after production code derives roots, counts, header history, and checked supply; it does not prove full action payload validation, tx proof soundness, PoW threshold validity, or complete native-node equivalence.

claims.summary: The Lean native tx-leaf artifact kernel proves executable parse facts for the bounded wire grammar, including fixed headers, serialized STARK/public-tx count caps, proof-length caps, commitment row caps, backend-byte defaults, bad backend rejection, trailing-byte rejection, and truncation rejection; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production superneo-hegemon decoder and canonical encoder.
assumptions: The Lean model covers the native tx-leaf artifact byte grammar and parser control flow, not lattice commitment security, STARK proof soundness, or statement-digest collision resistance.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover valid explicit-backend artifacts, missing-backend default behavior, legacy Plonky3 default behavior, trailing bytes, bad backend IDs, every bounded vector count, proof-length cap rejection, row/coeff caps, and truncation rejection.
The production conformance test checks decoder agreement and canonical re-encoding behavior in superneo-hegemon; consensus receipt binding and statement-hash semantics are covered by separate claims.

claims.component: native receipt-root artifact wire grammar and fold schedule
claims.summary: The Lean native receipt-root kernel proves executable parse and structural schedule facts for the bounded artifact grammar, including leaf/fold caps, trailing/truncation rejection, exact non-empty expected leaf agreement, fold_count = leaf_count - 1, exact fold challenge count, and exact fold row/coeff dimensions; generated Lean vectors are checked against the production superneo-hegemon decoder and structural validator used before backend fold verification.
assumptions: The Lean model covers native receipt-root artifact byte parsing and the cheap structural fold-schedule gate, not cryptographic validity of the lattice fold proof, transcript challenge derivation, or commitment binding security.

Generated Lean conformance vectors cover one-, two-, and three-leaf valid schedules plus zero expected leaves, leaf-count mismatch, missing/extra folds, bad challenge counts, bad row counts, bad row coefficient counts, over-cap leaf/fold counts, trailing bytes, and truncation.

The production conformance test checks decoder agreement, canonical re-encoding, and the structural validator now called by both native receipt-root verifier entry points before backend fold verification.

claims.summary: The Lean message-root kernel proves the bridge message-root transcript grammar and generated vectors check that production Rust hashes the same transcript; independent reference vectors still rederive payload hashes, message hashes, single-message roots, and inbound replay keys.
assumptions: The Lean MessageRoot model covers the byte transcript that is fed into BLAKE3 XOF-48, not BLAKE3 collision resistance or implementation equivalence.
Generated Lean conformance vectors cover empty, single-message, ordered pair, reversed pair, and invalid hash-length message-root transcript cases against protocol-kernel helpers.
The reference checker does not import protocol-kernel bridge helpers.
The vector set is updated when bridge wire semantics intentionally change.

claims.summary: The Lean replay-state kernel proves duplicate rejection for inbound replay staging and import, and generated Lean vectors are checked against the production protocol-kernel replay helper.
assumptions: The Lean ReplayKey equality model represents equality of canonical inbound replay keys.
Generated Lean conformance vectors cover protocol-kernel BridgeMessageV1 byte encoding and InboundReplayState stage/import behavior.
The theorem does not prove BLAKE3 replay-key derivation or full native-node implementation equivalence beyond the node's use of the protocol-kernel replay helper.

claims.component: native tx leaf and receipt root artifacts
claims.summary: Native backend proof artifacts are checked against fixed valid and invalid vectors by an independent reference verifier.
assumptions: The native-backend-ref verifier remains independent of production verification helpers.
The vector bundle includes malformed companions for canonical decode and binding failures.
The native tx-leaf artifact wire grammar is separately Lean-gated by formal.native-tx-leaf-artifact-wire.
The consensus native tx-leaf envelope/profile/size/hash/cache admission table is separately Lean-gated by consensus.native-tx-leaf-admission.
The native receipt-root artifact wire grammar and structural fold schedule are separately Lean-gated by formal.native-receipt-root-wire.

claims.summary: Release nodes reject RISC Zero bridge receipts after syntactic and message-binding prechecks until a PQ-clean verifier exists.
assumptions: Rejecting the receipt is safer than linking a classical verifier surface into the release node.

residual_risks.description: Hegemon-to-Hegemon inbound bridge receipt acceptance is disabled until a PQ-clean verifier replaces the offline experimental envelope.

claims.summary: The active native backend remains a candidate under external review rather than accepted production cryptography.
assumptions: The external review package and theorem notes are evidence, not machine-checked Lean proofs.
Release posture scripts must keep the candidate label unless accepted review artifacts are deliberately added.

residual_risks.description: The native backend reduction and aggregation theorem notes are not yet mechanized in Lean or another proof assistant.
